Who bathes before he eats and sleeps afte he eats him illness seldom overtakes. 3. Muṅh dhove rozī khove, nhāë narak meṅ jāë, Chaukā de-ke kare rasoī uske ghar kā rahe na koī . The apology of the unclean. Who'll wash his face will lose his place, Who bathes will surely go to Hell, Who ere he cooks a square will trace, No one will leave his name to tell. 4. Dūno ber jo ghūme phire, tīn kāl jo khāë, Sadā nirogī, changā rahe jo prātaë uṭh nhāë . Who walks out morn and eve, and eats three times a day, And bathes at early dawn will good health keep alway. nahānī, nhānī , Wom. adj. Impure (with menses). nahānī h. , v. n. 1. more: n. m. S. नयन Pr. naanam . The eye ( āṅkh , 1). 1. Nainā det batāë sab hiye ko het ahet, Jaise nirmal ārsī bhalī burī kah det . Brind satsāī. The eyes too clearly show the love and hatred of the breast, As in the clear glass faces fair and ugly are expressed. 2. Samujhāë samujhe nahīṅ, palak let nahīṅ chain, Nīr bhare piyāse raheṅ, nipaṭ anokhe nain . Dohā. Advise it it heeds not, the eyelids know no rest, Though wet it thirsty is, so strange the eye's unrest! 3. Chār kahār ḍuliye līye ṭhāṛe, saṅg prohit, nāī, Chalnā banego, ab hot kahā hai, nainā, nīr bahāī? A dirge. Four bearers standing with the bier, priest, barber both attend, Go now he must, in vain ye eyes you weep, your tears expend! 4. Chātur nār aur sūrmāṅ kareṅ lākh meṅ choṭ, Nain chhipāë nā chhipeṅ paṭ ghūṅgaṭ kī oṭ . Sharp women and brave men their hundred thousands slay, No veil can e'er conceal their eyes' incessant play.
s. m. The eye. نين سکهہ nain-sukh, s. m. Name of a flower.
wety: Semi-learned borrowing from Sanskrit नयन (nayana), from the root नी (nī), from Proto-Indo-European *neyH-.

more: s. f. The business of a carpenter, carpentry.
wety: Borrowed from Classical Persian نجاری (najjārī).

def: s.m. A star; planet; — fortune; — a prediction from observations of the stars; — a horoscope, calculation of nativity
more: n. m. ( In comp .) A star. Najm-ul-Hind , The star of India. ilm-i-najūm , n. m. Astrology ( jotish ).
s. m. 1. A star, a planet. 2. Fortune. 3. A horoscope, calculation of nativity. Plur. نجوم nujūm, Stars. علم نجوم iﻌlm-i-nujūm, Astrology, astronomy.
wety: Borrowed from Classical Persian نجم (najm), from Arabic نَجْم (najm).

def: s.f. A delicate woman; a term of endearment.
more: Hin. Poet. n. m. Cor. of نازک A delicate woman. Tan phūhaṛ kā bhaiṅs sūṅ bhārī, Kahe 'kaho mohe nājo piyārī!' Rus. As a buffalo fat, says the ninny to him, 'Call me, darling, your pet, lovely, delicate, slim!'

def: s.m. Gall-nut; oak-apple (cf. māzū); — mājū-phal, s.m. Idem (and the more com. form).
s.m. (dialec.) = nāj, q.v. s.m. Grain, corn
subs: To cut the corn; to reap the harvest
s.m. A wholesale dealer in grain or corn.

def: adj. corr. of نازك nāzuk, q.v. adj. Thin, slender, slim, delicate, tender, fragile; fine; light; brittle; nice; neat; elegant; genteel; subtle; — facetious; gracious; keen; sensitive, touchy, testy; — s.m. A species of jujube (i.q. nāzuk badan)
subs: adj. Having sweet notes (as the nightingale, &c.); delicate in performing or singing
s.f. Sweetness of note; delicacy of performance or singing
adj. Of delicate frame, delicate (epithet of a mistress); — s.m. A species of jujube, Zizyphus jujuba
s.f. Delicacy of body or of form
s.f. A tender part or spot; a vital part
s.m. A fine thought; delicate perception; nice judgment; — adj. & s.m. Having a fine perception; of delicate or nice ideas; — one who has a fine perception, &c.
s.m. A delicate or sensitive brain; a sensitive mind; — adj. & s.m. Having (or one who has) a sensitive brain or mind; — a touchy or testy person

s.m. A delicate constitution; — a sensitive disposition, or temper; — adj. & s.m. Of a delicate complexion or habit of body; — of a sensitive disposition or temper; — a person of delicate constitution or complexion, &c.; — a person of a touchy or testy disposition (syn. nāzuk-damāg̠)
s.f. Delicacy of complexion or habit of body, &c.
s.m. A delicate affair; a ticklish matter
or nāzuk zamāna, s.m. A critical moment; a crisis.

def: s.m. Astronomy; astrology.
wety: Borrowed from Classical Persian نجوم (nujūm), from Arabic نُجُوم (nujūm).

ety: Prk. नक्कं, or नक्का; S. नक्रं, or नक्रा
def: s.f. The nose; — (met.) a conspicuous or prominent person or thing; — honour; grace, ornament
subs: or nāk bahnā (-kī), To have a running at the nose
or nāk uṛānā (-kī), To take off, or to whip off, the nose (of); to disgrace (syn. nāk kāṭnā)
or sameṭnā, To turn up the nose and knit the brows
The nose to be or become flattened
(-kī), To pierce or bore the nose (of)
'To put the finger on the nose when speaking' (as is the manner of women); to act like a woman; to be effeminate
(-kisī-kī), 'A wheel to pass over the nose (of)'; the nose to become flat
'To place the money on the nose'; to pay readily (for a thing)
or nāk-par mizāj honā, 'To carry (one's) anger, or temper, at the tip of the nose'; to be very irascible
To turn up the nose (through contempt or pride); to show contempt (for, -par), to spurn; — to look angry, to be angry or displeased
(kisī-ko), 'To make one chew gram with the nose;' to torment, worry, harass
(with gen.), 'The nose and top-knot to be entangled or caught'; to be in great difficulties
or nāk-sākh rakhnā (apnī), 'To keep (one's) nose'; to preserve (one's) honour; to have a good name
To rub the nose (against the ground); — to beseech very humbly

'Hair of the nose'; — one who is respected or honoured; — one who has influence over another
(-kī), To cut off the nose (of); to disgrace, dishonour
To hold (or holding) before one the threat of cutting off the nose; duress by menaces or threats
(-ke), To cut off the nose and ears (of); to disgrace, dishonour
(apnī, or kisī-kī), To have or get the ears cut off; to disgrace, dishonour
or nāk kaṭā honā (-kī), The nose (of a person) to be cut off; to be disgraced
s.f. The line of the nose; — adv. In a straight line with the nose; straight as an arrow; as the crow flies
(with gen.), To rub the nose; — to twist the nose (of)
(-kī), 'To pierce (one's) nose with an arrow'; to worry, harass
(with gen.), 'The breath to come into (one's) nostrils'; to be greatly worried or harassed
(-kī), 'To cause the breath to come into the nostrils (of)'; to worry, plague, harass
To be unable to put the nose (into a house, on account of the stench)
s.m. (f. -wālī), 'One having a nose'; an honourable man; a person of rank
adj. & adv. Up to the nose, brimful, to the brim
(-ko), To fill to the nose; to stuff, cram
or suʼe-kī-sī nāk, A nose like a parrot's beak.
wety: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀡𑀓𑁆𑀓 (ṇakka), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *náHs, from Proto-Indo-European *néh₂s. def: s.f. Smell of the breath; — anything odoriferous; perfume, odour.

(i.q. S. नक्, base नज्), suffix (added to verbal roots, and subst., to form adj. of quality), Affected (with), inflamed (with), full (of), -ful; laden (with), &c. (e.g. ḵẖishm-nāk, 'inflamed with anger'; g̠am-nāk, 'affected with grief, sorrowful').
more: ( In comp .) Full; affected with, etc. as, gam-nāk full of grief.

n. f. S. नासिका. 1. The nose. 1. Nāk to haiye nā, nathiyā pahne ke sādh . Bhoj. Prov. She has no nose and she wants a nose-ring. 2. Muṅh se hajār chāur khāī, nāk se ekaū nā . Bhoj. Prov. With your mouth you may eat a thousand grains of rice, with your nose not one. 3. Nāk par supiyārī toṛte haiṅ . Prov. He cracks betel-nuts on his nose. (He is very irascible). 4. Nāk par dīyā bāl-kar āë ho . You are too late. 2. A conspicuous or prominent person or thing. 3. Honor; grace; ornament. nāk ānā, nāk bahnā , v. n. To flow (mucus) from the nose. nāk-baṅd , n. m. The nose-band. nāk bhauṅ chaṛhānā, yā sameṭnā , v. n. To turn up one's nose and knit the eyebrows. nāk baiṭhnā, yā pichaknā , v. n. To be flattened (one's nose). nāk bīṅdhnā, yā chhednā , v. a. To bore one's nose. nāk par uṅglī rakh-kar bāt k. , v. n. lit . to put one's finger on the nose (as women do). To be effeminate. nāk par pahiyā …

s. f. The nose. ناک چڙهانا nāk chaṛhānā, To be angry or displeased. ناک رکهنا nāk rakhnā, To preserve oneʼs honour. ناک سکوڙنا nāk sakoṛnā, To turn up oneʼs nose, to be displeased. ناک کا بال nāk kā bāl, adj. (dakh. met. ) Respected, honoured: see عزيز aﻌzīz. ناک کٿانا nāk kaṭānā, To dishonour. ناک کٿي هوني nāk kaṭī honī, To lose oneʼs honour.
(in compos.) Full, affected with, &c. as, غمناک g̠ẖam-nāk, Affected with grief.

def: contrac. of nāk, 'nose' (used in compounds which will be found below).
more: n. f. Cont. of nāk nose, q. v. nak-bāl , n. m. The hair in the nose. nak-besar , A nose-ring. nak-toṛā , n. m. Sneer; arrogance. nak-toṛe toṛnā, nak nak k. , v. n. To cast in one's teeth (an obligation). nak-chaṛhā, nak-chaṛhī , adj. 1. Turned up (nose). 2. Nice; fastidious. 3. Fretful; ill-tempered. 4. Imperious; haughty. nak-chhiknī , n. f. Artemisia sternutatoria , Wat. nak-sīr , n. f. Bleeding at the nose. nak-sīr phūṭnā, chalnā, yā chhūṭnā , To bleed at the nose.
1. in compos. (for ناک nāk, q. v.) Nose; as, نک گهسني nak-ghisnī, s. Rubbing the nose on the ground in prostration, or by way of humiliation. 2. s. (dakh.) Nail: see نکهـ nakh.

def: s.m. corr. of 1˚ nakh, 'nail,' q.v.; — 2˚ naḵẖ, q.v. s.m. A thread (of yarn, or silk, &c.); silk thread (for flying a kite); — a kind of carpet with long pile; — rank, row, line (of soldiers, and the like); — a little; a few (cf. S. nakha).

s.m. Extremity (of a road, &c.); point of meeting (of two or more roads); entrance (to a road, or a pass, &c.); a boundary; — eye (of a needle); — opening, or gap (in a bank, or hedge or fence); — a passage, lane, an alley, avenue; — a water-way; — a toll-station, customs' station; — a passage or gate through a customs' line; — a subordinate police station; post or beat (of a constable)
more: n. m. 1. The entrance to a pass or road. 2. ( nakvā ) The eye of a needle. 3. An opening in a bank, hedge, or fence. 4. A toll or customs' station. 5. A waterway. 6. A subordinate police station. 7. E. The post or beat of a constable. 8. A gate or passage through the customs' line. nākā-bandī , n. f. 1. Placing a picquet on a road. 2. Collections on account of land customs and transit duties. nāke-dār , adj. Having an eye (a needle). nāke-dār , n. m. The receiver of customs or transit duties. nāke meṅ ko nikālnā , v. a. 1. To pass a thread through the eye of a needle. 2. To coerce; subjugate.
s. m. 1. The extremity of a road. 2. The eye of a needle. 3. An alley, avenue, lane. ناکي بندي nāke-bandī, Shutting up a road.
subs: s.f. Shutting up a road; placing a picket at the extremity of a road; — collecting (or collections on account of) land customs and transit-duties
adj. Having an eye (a needle); — s.m. The receiver of customs or transit duties
v.t. To pass (a thread) the eye of a needle; — to bring under the yoke, to subjugate; to coerce.

def: adj. (dakh. for ناقدردان) Not knowing worth, disregarder of merit.
Poet. n. f. A she-camel ( ūṅṭnī ).

more: (from نقه) s. f. 1. Recovering from disease, convalescence. 2. Imbecility, feebleness, languidness, languor.

def: n. f. Weakness; debility.
(v.n. fr. نکل 'to receive chastisement'), s.m.(?) Exemplary punishment; a preventive, restraint; — a public and ignominious exposure.
more: (from نکل Heb. and Aram. ) s. m. An exemplary punishment, a preventive, restraint.

def: s. (dakh.) A colt ( Harris ): see ناکند nā-kand, under نا nā.
adj. See s.v. nā. adv. (In Urdū) The inseparable form of نه na, q.v

ety: inf. n. of نقب 'to dig (through a wall
def: s.f. Digging through a wall, house-breaking (syn. seṅdh, q.v.); — a subterraneous excavation; a mine (under a wall); — a (rabbit's) burrow; — a mine, a gallery; — a crow-bar or other implement used by miners, house-breakers, &c. (syn. seṅdh-kātī)
more: n. f. 1. A mine under a wall. 2. A crowbar used by miners, house-breakers, etc. ( sendh-kātī ). 3. A rabbit's burrow. naqab denā yā lagānā, naqab-zanī k. , v. a. To break into (a house). naqab-zan , n. m. A burglar. naqab-zanī , n. f. House-breaking; burglary.
s. f. 1. A rabbitʼs burrow, a subterraneous excavation, perforation, a mine, a gallery. 2. An instrument used by miners, thieves, &c. for piercing walls. 3. Digging through a wall.
subs: or naqab lagānā (-meṅ), To make an excavation under (the wall of a house), to break into (a house)
s.m. A miner; a house-breaker, burglar
To break into (a house), to commit burglary (i.q. naqab denā).

def: s.m. The hair of the nose.
s. (dakh.) Nose-band (of a bridle: Harris ).

def: s.f. Adversity, calamity, misfortune, affliction.
more: (from نکب) s. f. Adversity, calamity, misfortune.

def: s.m. An ornament worn in the nose (by women), a heavy nose-ring.

def: adj. (f. -ī), Having the nose turned up; — fastidions, nice; — disdainful, proud, haughty; — fretful; ill-tempered; warm, angry, passionate.
adj. Angry, fretful, ill-tempered, warm, passionate, fastidious.
s.f. The plant sneeze-wort, Artemisia sternutatoria.
more: s. f. Name of a sternutatory plant.

def: n. f. Small pincers ( mochnā ).

ety: inf. n. of نقد 'to count out, or pay in, ready money'
def: adj. & s.m. Prompt, or ready (payment); — good, fine, choice (article or goods); — of a just standard (coin); — ready money, cash; — (met.) a fine fellow; (in jest, or ridicule) a son-in-law (so called because he receives money from his father-in-law when he visits him)
more: adj. 1. Prompt or ready (payment). 2. Good; fine. Naqad māl . naq'ad , n. m. 1. ( naqdī ) Ready money; cash. Naqduhu hurmatahu . Prov. Pay cash, your honor keep. 2. A nice fellow. Tum to baṛe naqad āë . 3. A son-in-law, so called because he receives money from his father-in-law when he visits him. Ham to tumhāre naqad haiṅ. naqdā naqd , Prompt payment. naqd dam rahnā , v. n. To live in single blessedness. naqad māl , Dainties; tit bits. naqd o jins , Money and goods. naqdī jinsī , Money rent, calculated on the value of the produce. naqd'ī chhiṭṭhā , n. m. Cash account ( khātā rokaṛ ). naqdī faislā , n. m. A settlement of cash balances. naqdī gumāshta , n. m. A cash-keeper ( khazānchī ). sarmāyā-naqdī , G. G. Funded property.
s. m. Ready money, cash. نقدا نقد naḳd-ā-naḳd, Prompt payment.
subs: v.n. To live a single life, to remain a bachelor
s.m. Good or choice article or goods; — a dainty, tit-bit

more: adj. Relating to cash, (paid, &c.) in cash, possessing ready money, moneyed. ¶ نقدي کا جوڙا naḳde kā joṛā (dakh.) An ornament of bangles for the wrist.
subs: s.f.(?) Money rent (calculated on the value of the produce)
s.m. A subordinate in a treasury; — a cash-keeper (syn. ḵẖazānćī).

def: s.f. The wooden or iron pin fixed in a camel's nose, and to which his halter is fastened; a cavesson; — the halter by which a camel is led, and which is fastened to a pin through his nose.
more: n. f. Cont. of nāk nose, and kīl nail. S. नासिका कील. . 1. A wooden pin passed through the camel's nose. 2. The string to guide a camel, tied to the wooden pin. Unkī nakel to mere hāth hai .
s. f. The wooden or iron instrument fixed to a camelʼs nose, and to which the string by which he is led is fastened; a cavesson.

s.m. A thread (of yarn, or silk, &c.); silk thread (for flying a kite); — a kind of carpet with long pile; — rank, row, line (of soldiers, and the like); — a little; a few (cf. S. nakha).
more: n. m. Silk thread used for flying kites.

def: s.m. Nail (of a finger or toe); a claw, talon; — (in Poetry) a symbol for the number twenty (the number of the nails); — a kind of perfume (a dried substance of a brown colour, and of the shape of a nail. or of a pared nail)

def: From top to toe, or from the (toe) nails to the hair on the crown of the head.

s.m. See s.v. nakh. s.m. A thread (of yarn, or silk, &c.); silk thread (for flying a kite); — a kind of carpet with long pile; — rank, row, line (of soldiers, and the like); — a little; a few (cf. S. nakha).

more: s. m. The chase, game, prey, hunting. نخچير گاه naḵẖchīr-gāh, s. f. Place of hunting.
wety: Borrowed from Classical Persian نَخْچِیر (naxčīr, “hunt, hunting; game, quarry; prey”). Cognate with Parthian 𐭍𐭇𐭔𐭉𐭓 (naxčīr, “game, quarry, chase”), Sogdian ܢܓܫܝܪ (naxšīr, “wild beast; wild goat”), Wakhi نخچیر (naxčīr, “fox”), Yidgha نخشیر (naxšīr, “goat”), Yaghnobi нахшир (naxšir), Sanskrit नश्चीरपति (naścīrapati), and Gandhari 𐨣𐨐𐨿𐨕𐨁𐨌𐨪 (naxcīra).

def: adj. & s.m. (f. -inī), Having nails; armed with claws or talons, clawed; — an animal or a beast with talons or claws.
s.f. A kind of perfume (i.q. nakh, q.v.); — (dialec.) the quill, or the wire-ring worn on the finger, with which the strings of a musical instrument are struck.

ety: nakhiyā˚ = nakhī ( = S. nakha+ikā)+ā = āw = Prk. आवे or आव = S. आपि (caus. aug.)
def: v.t. To scratch, to claw.
more: v. a. To claw, to scratch.

ety: intens. n. of نخس 'to drive, spur, goad,' &c.
def: s.m. (orig.) 'A seller of slaves or cattle taken in plunder'; (in Urdū) a slave-mart; horse-mart; cattle-market; — a tax levied (in some of the Native States) on the sale of horses and cattle
more: n. m. 1. A horse or slave market. Ghar ghoṛā, nakhās mol! Prov. The horse left in his stall, to sell him for the market bound. 2. A tax, levied in some of the Native States, on the sale of horses and cattle. nakhās par bhejnā , To expose for public sale. nakhkhās kī ghoṛī, lit . a mare on public sale. A frail woman. nakhās-vāliyāṅ , n. f. Prostitutes.
s. m. A market for slaves and cattle.
subs: To send to the mart, to expose for public sale.

def: s.m. Date-tree, palm-tree; (in P. & Urdū, also) a tree, a sapling, a plant; — a festoon, a thyrsus, a rod upon which flowers, interspersed with paper of different colours, are tied round to give it the appearance of a natural branch; an artificial tree
more: s. m. A date tree, a palm tree (and often used for a young tree of any kind), a plant. نخل بند naḵẖl-band, s. m. A gardener, a maker of artificial trees and fruits of wax. نخلبندي naḵẖl-band-ī, A plantation.
subs: s.m. A gardener; — a maker of festoons, or of artificial trees and flowers
s.m. A grove of palms, or of other trees; a plantation.

ety: for A. نخلة naḵẖlat, fr. naḵẖl
def: s.m. A palm-grove; a grove; a plantation.
(per met. for lakhnau), prop. n. The city of Lakhnau (or Lucknow).

ety: Prk. नखउल्लओ and नखउल्लिआ; S. नख+उल+कः, and इका
def: (dialec.) The bandage of the fingers of a corpse; — a bit of dough rolled upon the nail (to be thrown into khīr, &c.); — a kind of ornament (worn by children).

def: s. f. The marks left by the nails, a scratch.
s.f. = H نکهسکهہ नखसिख nakh-sikh, adv. See s.v. nakh.

prop. n. Name of a city in Turkistān, famous for the appearance of the moon which the impostor Muqannaʻ caused to ascend from a pit in the neighbourhood (called ćāh-ě-naḵẖshab) during the space of two months.
more: n. prop. Name of a city in Turkistan, famous for the appearance of the moon which the impostor Muḳannaﻌ caused to ascend from a pit in its neighbourhood, during the space of two months.

def: s. m. (dakh.) see رخساره ruḵẖsāra.
adv. = H نکهريکهہ नखरेख nakh-rekh, s.f. See s.v. nakh.
s.m. See s.v. نا nā. adv. (In Urdū) The inseparable form of نه na, q.v
more: n. m. Cont. of ناو boat, خدا master. The captain or supercargo of a ship.
(comp. of ناو and خدا) s. m. Master or commander of a ship, captain, or supercargo.
wety: Borrowed from Classical Persian ناخدا (nāxudā), from earlier ناوخدا (nāwxudā, literally “ship-master”).

def: s.m. The master, or commander, of a ship; captain; — a supercargo; — a seaman.
wety: Borrowed from Classical Persian ناخدا (nāxudā), from earlier ناوخدا (nāwxudā, literally “ship-master”).

def: s.f. Office or status of a ship-captain, &c.; — seamanship, navigation.
s. f. Office of master, &c. of a ship, navigation.

r: nāḵẖun, nāḵẖ˚, un, ūn, wan, wān
ety: nāḵẖ˚ = S. नख+un = ūn = wan = wān = S. वान
def: s.m. Nail (of the finger or toe); talon, claw
more: n. m. Lat. unguis , S. नख Finger or toe nail; talons; claw. 1. Rīchh ke se nākhūn baṛhā rakkhe haiṅ . 2. Hāṛ kī dehī, ujjal raṅg, lipaṭ rahe nārī ke saṅg, Nā mārā, nā-khūn kiyā; bin pūchhe sir kāṭ līyā . Riddle. Ans. Nākhūn . Finger nail. As white as a bone, he a female clasps free, Nor slew nor blood drew, forced, beheaded lies he. 3. Rizāle ke nākhūn hue! Prov. Base men now have claws. (Unworthy men are raised to high positions). nākhūn se likhnā , v. a. To write with one's nail (held an accomplishment). nākhun-gīr, nākhun-tarāsh , n. m. A nail-parer. nākhun lenā , v. n. 1. To pare the nails. 2. To trip or stumble. nākhun meṅ paṛe rahnā , v. n. To have in one's pocket. Tum jaise to mere nākhūnoṅ meṅ paṛe haiṅ .

s. m. Nail (of the finger, toe, &c.) talon, claw. ناخن گير يا تراش nāḵẖūn-gīr, or -tarāsh, s. m. An instrument for paring the nails with. ناخن لينا nāḵẖun lenā, 1. To pare the nails. 2. To trip or stumble (a horse).
subs: v.t. To write with the finger-nail (considered an accomplishment)
or nāḵẖun-tarāsh, s.m. A small knife, or scissors, for paring the nails
v.t. To pare the nails; — to trip, or stumble (a horse)
v.n. To be in (one's) possession; to be lying in the pocket.
wety: Borrowed from Classical Persian نَاخُن (nāxun).

def: s.m. = P ناخونه nāḵẖūna, s.m. i.q. ناخن nāḵẖun; and ناخنه nāḵẖuna, qq.v.

s.m. An instrument used to strike the strings of a lute (or guitar, &c.) with, a plectrum; a bow (i.q. miẓrāb); — a haw, or web (in the eye), cataract; — a kind of cloth made of silk and cotton; — enmity, malice.
more: n. m. 1. A haw or web in the eye. (?) Uskī āṅkh meṅ nākhūnā paṛ gayā . 2. A kind of plectrum for striking the strings of a guitar, etc. 3. A cloth made of silk and cotton. 4. Enmity; malice.
s. m. 1. An instrument used to strike the strings of a guitar, &c. with, a plectrum. 2. A haw or web in the eye. (Unguis).
